ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Kothu Prata or Kothu Parotta. FOODON_00002700 (flatbread food product) is used as the closest parent. A more specific term covering chopped flatbread stir-fried with meat is absent from the ontology.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific node for Kothu Prata. A0FKP (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible parent category covering flatbread-based mixed dishes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Kothu Prata or analogous chopped flatbread-meat dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Mutton Kothu Prata is approximately 350 g, typically presented as a single plate of chopped flatbread stir-fried with mutton, egg, and curry sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Kothu Parotta (mutton) — nutrient composition estimated from ICMR-NIN component data: Parotta / Roti Canai and cooked mutton curry, combined with USDA SR Legacy values for egg and onion
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Mutton Kothu Prata as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for parotta (layered flatbread) and mutton curry with USDA SR Legacy data for egg and onion, weighted by typical recipe proportions (approximately 45% flatbread, 35% mutton, 10% egg, 10% aromatics and sauce). Energy cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo analogous mixed Indian flatbread-meat dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of roti canai / parotta and mixed flatbread dishes — analogous estimate based on roti canai GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on South Asian flatbreads
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Kothu Prata. GI estimated at 57 based on roti canai / parotta GI values (range 54–62) reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed studies on South Asian layered flatbreads. The addition of mutton protein and fat is expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain parotta; the estimate of 57 reflects the mixed-dish composition. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 75.25) / 100 ≈ 43.0 g, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 21.5 × 3.5 = 75.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per standard 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Mutton Kothu Prata to occasional consumption and request reduced-salt preparation, as a single serving can contribute approximately 64% of the WHO-recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~43 g GL) driven by refined-flour flatbread base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat Mutton Kothu Prata as an occasional food due to its high glycemic load; if consumed, portion size should be restricted to approximately 150–175 g and paired with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~3.8 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium; mutton contributes cholesterol (~62 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit Mutton Kothu Prata to occasional consumption, as the combined saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, and high sodium content are unfavourable for lipid profiles and blood pressure control.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Kothu Prata is a popular South Indian and Singaporean Indian dish made by finely chopping cooked prata (roti canai — a layered, flaky flatbread of South Indian origin) on a hot iron griddle and stir-frying the pieces together with tender mutton pieces, beaten egg, sliced onion, curry leaves, green chilli, and a rich curry sauce. The dish is cooked at high heat with vigorous chopping and folding motions using metal spatulas, producing a characteristic rhythmic clanging sound that is part of its street-food identity. The texture is a satisfying combination of slightly crispy flatbread edges, soft interior pieces, and juicy mutton, all coated in a fragrant, spiced curry gravy. The flavour profile is bold, savoury, and moderately spicy, with aromatic notes from curry leaves and whole spices. In Singapore, Mutton Kothu Prata is served at Indian Muslim hawker stalls and restaurants, often accompanied by additional curry sauce or raita. Nutritionally, it is a calorie-dense mixed dish providing approximately 218 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein from mutton (~10.5 g per 100 g), moderate fat (~10.2 g per 100 g including ~3.8 g saturated fat), and refined carbohydrate from the flatbread base (~21.5 g per 100 g). Sodium content is high (~420 mg per 100 g), making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. The glycemic load per standard serving is high, requiring caution for people with Type 2 Diabetes. Iron content is notable (~2.1 mg per 100 g) due to the mutton component. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Mutton Kothu Prata?
What is the glycemic index of Kothu Prata?
Is Kothu Prata healthy for diabetics?

Can I eat Kothu Prata if I have high blood pressure?
What are the main ingredients in Mutton Kothu Prata?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Mutton Kothu Prata as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for parotta and mutton curry with USDA SR Legacy data for egg and aromatics, weighted by typical recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on roti canai / parotta GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from ICMR-NIN (parotta, mutton curry) and USDA SR Legacy (egg, onion), weighted at approximately 45% flatbread, 35% mutton, 10% egg, 10% aromatics and sauce. Direct laboratory analysis of this composite dish is not available in any approved source. 2. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on roti canai / parotta GI data (range 54–62) from the University of Sydney GI Database; the protein and fat contribution of mutton is expected to modestly suppress the effective GI of the composite dish. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 6. trans_fat_g is set to 0.1 reflecting minor trans fat from the oil used in frying the flatbread, consistent with ICMR-NIN data for parotta. 7. Halal status reflects standard preparation at Indian Muslim establishments in Singapore; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. The food_group is retained as 'Meat and alternatives' from the input record, consistent with the mutton being the primary protein component, though the dish is a mixed flatbread-meat preparation.
